basics.name: Dr Alan Ruddock
basics.label: Associate Professor of Sport Physiology and Performance
basics.summary: Associate Professor of Sport Physiology and Performance at Sheffield Hallam University and Co-Founder of Boxing Science. CASES Fellow, Chartered Scientist, and Senior Fellow of the HEA. 50+ peer-reviewed publications (h-index: 25) spanning exercise physiology, high-intensity interval training, thermoregulation, and combat sports science. Led scientific support for boxers in 15 world-title contests including Anthony Joshua OBE. Recipient of the CASES Applied Practitioner Award 2021.

work.name: Chartered Association of Sport and Exercise Sciences (CASES)
work.position: Director & Chair of Physiology and Nutrition Division
highlights: Providing strategic leadership for the Physiology and Nutrition Division
Overseeing professional standards and accreditation processes
Contributing to the governance and direction of the organisation

work.name: Sheffield Hallam University
work.position: Associate Professor of Sport Physiology and Performance
highlights: Course Leader for BSc Sport Technology and BSc Sport and Exercise Science (2021-2025), managing up to 350 students and 30 staff

work.name: Boxing Science
highlights: Provides scientific support to boxers and combat athletes worldwide
Led scientific support for boxers in 15 world-title contests
Delivered workshops to over 500 coaches across UK, Europe, North America and Asia
Platform reaches 30,000 unique users per month

work.name: Sheffield Hallam University
work.position: Ethics Lead, School of Sport and Physical Activity
highlights: Leading research ethics governance for taught programmes
Ensuring ethical compliance across student research projects

work.name: Sheffield Hallam University
work.position: Laboratory Director, School of Sport and Physical Activity
highlights: Oversaw research and teaching laboratory activities

work.name: Birmingham Newman University
highlights: Ensured academic standards and quality assurance
Reviewed assessment processes and student outcomes
